audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 16, first-pass restored draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Maximum audit for Samuel's grief and safety fear, sacrificial concealment, trembling elders, appearance/heart contrast, David's age and absence, private anointing, Spirit transfer, harmful-spirit theology, music relief, royal service, armor-bearing, and possible double-introduction traditions.

verses.restored: YHWH said to Samuel, How long will you grieve over Saul, when I have rejected him from reigning over Israel? Fill your horn with oil and go; I will send you to Jesse the Bethlehemite, for I have seen among his sons a king for myself.
Samuel said, How can I go? Saul will hear and kill me. YHWH said, Take a heifer in your hand and say, I have come to sacrifice to YHWH.
Call Jesse to sacrifice, and I will make known to you what you shall do; you shall anoint for me whom I say to you.
Samuel did what YHWH spoke and came to Bethlehem. Elders of city trembled to meet him and said, Is your coming peace?
He said, Peace. I have come to sacrifice to YHWH. Sanctify yourselves and come with me to sacrifice. He sanctified Jesse and his sons and called them to sacrifice.
It happened when they came that he saw Eliab and said, Surely before YHWH is his anointed.

YHWH said to Samuel, Do not look toward his appearance or height of his stature, because I have rejected him; for it is not as human sees: human sees according to eyes, but YHWH sees according to heart.
Jesse called Abinadab and made him pass before Samuel. He said, This one also YHWH has not chosen.
Jesse made Shammah pass, and he said, This one also YHWH has not chosen.
Jesse made seven of his sons pass before Samuel, and Samuel said to Jesse, YHWH has not chosen these.
Samuel said to Jesse, Are all young men complete? He said, The youngest remains, and look, he is shepherding sheep. Samuel said to Jesse, Send and take him, for we will not sit until he comes here.
He sent and brought him. He was ruddy, with beautiful eyes and good appearance. YHWH said, Arise, anoint him, for this is he.
Samuel took horn of oil and anointed him in midst of his brothers. Spirit of YHWH rushed upon David from that day onward. Samuel arose and went to Ramah.
Spirit of YHWH turned aside from Saul, and a harmful spirit from YHWH terrified him.
Saul's servants said to him, Look, please, a harmful spirit of Divine is terrifying you.
Let our lord command your servants before you to seek a man who knows how to play lyre. When harmful spirit of Divine is upon you, he will play with his hand, and it will be well for you.
Saul said to his servants, See, please, for me a man who plays well, and bring him to me.

One of young men answered and said, Look, I have seen a son of Jesse the Bethlehemite who knows playing, a mighty man of strength, a man of war, discerning in speech, a man of form, and YHWH is with him.
Saul sent messengers to Jesse and said, Send to me David your son who is with sheep.
Jesse took a donkey loaded with bread, a skin of wine, and one young goat, and sent them by hand of David his son to Saul.
David came to Saul and stood before him. Saul loved him greatly, and he became bearer of his armor.
Saul sent to Jesse, saying, Let David stand before me, please, for he has found favor in my eyes.
It happened whenever spirit of Divine was upon Saul, David took lyre and played with his hand. Saul had relief, it was well for him, and harmful spirit turned aside from upon him.

translation_notes: Samuel's grief is not condemned as weakness; the question redirects him while acknowledging a real rupture.
Samuel fears Saul will kill him. Prophets and officials are not protected from political violence, and prudent safety planning is not faithlessness.
The sacrificial explanation functions as concealment within danger and requires ethical audit; it is not a general license for deception.
Bethlehem's elders tremble at Samuel's arrival, showing how religious authority can generate fear even without stated threat.
Verse 7 rejects appearance and height as reliable leadership measures, though verse 12 still describes David attractively; the narrative tension should remain.
David is the youngest and initially absent because he is working. Birth order and invisibility do not determine worth.
Private anointing does not authorize a child or young person to seize power, enter war, or accept unsafe adult expectations.
'Harmful spirit from YHWH' is ancient theological language. It must never diagnose mental illness, epilepsy, trauma, neurodivergence, or emotional distress as demonic, sinful, or Divine rejection.
Saul's distress is real and terrifying. He remains a person deserving care, privacy, and protection from stigma.
Music provides relief in the story but is not a substitute for professional medical or mental-health care when such care is needed.
David enters Saul's service through royal request to Jesse within a hierarchy. Favor and affection do not erase power imbalance or establish unrestricted consent.
Armor-bearing can expose a young servant to military danger; this role must not romanticize child soldiers.
The chapter's two introductions of David may reflect distinct traditions and require source-compositional audit.
safety_note: Do not use harmful-spirit language to stigmatize mental illness, trauma, disability, neurodivergence, seizures, or emotional distress. Do not tell people music alone replaces healthcare. Do not romanticize secret anointing, child soldiers, political deception, or a ruler's control over a young worker. Saul deserves humane care even after rejection.
